summary: Cathy O'Neil defends her habit of assuming everyone has a crush on her, explaining it as a reciprocal and non-sexual appreciation for others. She argues this mindset creates positive feedback loops in social interactions, making people more likely to actually develop fondness for you. The post playfully reframes what others see as preposterous self-confidence as a practical social strategy. Her core argument is that assuming positive regard from others costs nothing and creates a more enjoyable world to inhabit.
key_points: Having a 'crush' in this context means finding someone super awesome, not romantic or sexual attraction
Acting like someone has a crush on you creates a positive feedback loop that makes it more likely to become true
The worst-case scenario is just that someone thinks you were weirdly happy to see them
The assumption is reciprocal - she also has crushes on everyone else
This mindset transforms everyday social interactions into more pleasant experiences
money_quotes: First of all, I also have a crush on everyone else.
No categories left uncrushed except meanies.
When you act like someone has a crush on you, they are more likely to develop a crush on you.
It's what I call a positive feedback loop.
It's a nice world to live in where a majority of the people you run into have a crush on you.
Try it and see for yourself!
key_insight: Assuming positive regard from others is a self-fulfilling prophecy that costs nothing and makes social life more enjoyable.
